description: Figure 8 presents a multi-panel analysis of austenite stability and microstructural heterogeneity for intercritically annealed low-density medium-Mn steels M1 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-0.2C) and M2 (Fe-8Mn-3Al-1Si-0.2C). Panels (a1) and (a2) display the calculated retained austenite fraction at room temperature (left y-axis, solid squares) and the corresponding Ms temperatures (right y-axis, open squares) as functions of intercritical annealing temperature (600–900 °C), obtained using the Koistinen-Marburger model. A critical temperature near 650 °C is evident: in the shaded lower-temperature regime the Ms remains below room temperature, permitting complete retention of the equilibrium austenite fraction, whereas above this threshold the rising Ms promotes partial austenite-to-martensite transformation upon cooling. Panel (b) compares the grain size distribution of M1 and M2, showing that M1-IA contains a higher frequency of fine grains below 0.5 μm, which enhances local austenite stability. Panel (c) provides a schematic of elemental partitioning during intercritical annealing, where Mn and C partition from α-ferrite into austenite (γ) in both steels (red arrows), while M2 additionally exhibits Si partitioning from austenite into δ-ferrite and α-ferrite (black arrows), with arrow density indicating partitioning extent. Collectively, the figure rationalizes why the experimentally observed retained austenite fractions after IA at 700 °C (~45 % for M1 and ~19 % for M2) substantially exceed the K-M calculated values (~16 % and ~11 %), attributing the discrepancy to the combined effects of sub-micron grain refinement and local solute segregation.
